It has some phony data in it.
And one of the things you see here on the dashboard is software.
Now, one thing to understand about Fleet, and this is true of pretty much every MDM platform, is that all the devices that are managed report back their software inventory.
So what we're really seeing here is all the software that's installed on all of the devices.
But we also have a vulnerability management capability in Fleet.
So you can see which of the software titles that have been reported back to inventory are vulnerable.
And you can see here there's some version of Python that's vulnerable.
There's some version of Safari that's showing it's vulnerable.
So that's kind of cool that the vulnerability data is really very much up front in the product.
And one thing that's interesting about Fleet in comparison to Kanji.

It some very sophisticated vulnerability management features now, but one thing that's interesting about Fleet is that you can use it alongside another MDM if you want to, which is part of the reason for the confusion around the product, actually, that Fleet maybe isn't an MDM, because before Fleet had MDM features, like, we have lots of customers that use Jamf or lots of customers that use other MDMs, and they simply use Fleet for data collection.
Because OS Query is so powerful.
So, you might see an instance of Fleet that has MDM features enabled, or you might see one that doesn't.
That's a perfectly normal thing.

You're still going to see vulnerability data for software, because even if your devices aren't being managed by Fleet, there's still a Fleet agent that's installed that's collecting the inventory data.
Hopefully that makes sense.
That The other thing you see here on the dashboard is this activity feed.
And this is actually pretty cool.
Lots of web apps have something like this, Jira, if you've ever seen that, Confluence, lots of tools, kind of give you an up-to-the-minute status of what's happening in the platform.
Here, this shows events like devices that have been enrolled, scripts that have been run, when a GitOps push has been done, when someone logged in.
In the background, this is actually referred to as the audit log.
So this is all of the events and activities that are being audited in terms of access and actions.
And what's pretty cool about this is that this activity feed emits webhooks.
And this is something that you see throughout the product.
This is just one place that you see it.
So what this means is...
If I configure this webhook listener for, and it can be anything, it can be a generic webhook listener, it could be something like OctaWorkflows or Tynes, some sort of integration.

If you've got something that's configured to listen for a webhook, like Tynes or like OctaWorkflows or some other no-code, low-code platform, or it could be anything, it could be a full-on different web application.
What that means is that these webhooks can trigger events in those other systems.

So you can get a workflow like on an enrollment, do something else in Tynes to talk to some two-factor authentication solution like Duo, or talk to the HR system, or send a message in Slack that a new computer was enrolled to the IT team.
It's a very, very powerful system because, and it's not because other solutions don't have features like this, it's because it's generic.
It's that all it
What doing is sending a webhook, and that you can use that webhook however you need to, rather than being restricted into a feature for how that action might be used.

And a lot of other platforms are way more prescriptive about that kind of thing, and hopefully you'll see this as a theme throughout the product, that one of the things that our customers like about the product is this kind of generic extensibility.
For the more sophisticated the client platform engineering team is, or the security team is, the more interesting these features become to them.

For customers that aren't as sophisticated, those features might seem like barriers, but for people that know what to do with these things, it would be the difference between maybe your, and this is not meant to insult Kanji, but it's like the Duplo Legos versus the actual Legos versus the Masterworks Legos, right?
It's a very, very apt analogy.
Everybody knows what to do with the Duplo Legos.
Those are for three-year-olds.
Not everybody knows what to do with the Erector Set.
But if you know what to do with the Erector Set, you can do some incredible stuff.
So that's an analogy I like to use.
Okay.
In Fleet, we have this concept of teams.
This is kind of the top-level organizational unit.
You can think of a team really however you like.
Now, I don't know that I can show you this very well here.
Yeah.
Okay.
So there's two Windows computers and a Mac.
And that's important.
And this goes back to what I was saying on the last call with...
Ada.
With Ada.
Sorry.
Is that how you pronounce it?
Ada?
How you do?
That's the American way to say it, yeah.
Is it AIDA?
Yep.
Okay.
So the idea here is that you have multiple platforms in the team.
Well, why would you do that?
Because we believe that if you're simplifying things, there should be kind of a flatness across all of your platforms.
Theoretically, you should be able to manage your Windows computers the same way you manage your Macs.
Now, this isn't always true, but that's the theory.
So, and I'll talk more about that when I get to the control section.
But the idea here is that your teams can have, there's no reason to segregate your Windows devices from your Macs in separate teams unless you want to do that.
But the idea of a team is really more like, it's something that could function as a baseline.
So, all of the devices that you want to have the same set of
controls, you would have those in a single team.
And that might be your accounting department, or that might be your software development department, right?
But the thing about your accounting department is that those people all might have Windows computers.
So you might end up, if you're breaking up your teams that way, that you would have a single platform in the team.
It doesn't matter.
The point is that a team should be a group of devices that you want managed to the same way.
You can easily transfer a device from team to team.
It's super easy to do.
This is also where you can add hosts.
And this is going to seem a little confusing, but because what you're getting is a giant command here, where do you run that command?
So, a little bit, I think it's a little bit unusual for products like this.
Fleet has a...
So on binary for managing the console, and a lot of people are confused by this because what they see here is that, oh, this is the way you add hosts.
This must mean I need this on every one of my hosts.
In fact, what this is telling you to do is install a binary on your computer as the admin so that you can build packages for your hosts.
So Fleet CTL is a command line tool that lets you do a lot of really cool things for managing the console.
It's not something that gets enrolled on every host.
It's something that gets enrolled only or only gets installed, sorry, on an admin's computer for doing management things.
And one of the things you can do is build an enrollment package.
And you can see you can build the enrollment package for Windows or Linux or Chrome or iPad or Android or what have you.
Every team has a unique enrollment secret that in calling it a secret sounds scary.
This is really just
Kind of a unique identifier for the team.
That's how the database keeps track of the separate teams in the background.
And the output of this command when you have Fleet CTL installed on your computer is just a normal Apple PKG like any other.
For Windows, you would be getting an MSI, which is kind of the equivalent of a PKG on Mac.
For Linux, you can specify that you want a Debian package or an RPM package.
So this builds the type of installer package that is typical for the platform.
Gotcha.
Cool.
So if I go to controls now, what you'll see is that if I switch through these teams, the controls would be different for every team.
And this is what I'm talking about when I'm saying that you want a team.
The paradigm for a team is that it's a group of devices that you want to manage together.
it.

Because each team has its own set of controls, and each team has its own software, and each team has its own queries, and each team has its own policies.
And we'll talk about what these things are, okay?
There's one other thing to say about teams.
We have this idea of no team.
And this is also a little confusing.
But no team means that you enrolled a device in Fleet and didn't specify a team for it to be in.

In other words, if I made an enrollment package here, if I went to hosts, and I say, I want to add hosts for the best team, this command is going to create an enrollment package with this enrollment secret.
But there is a global enrollment secret.

So if a device gets enrolled, and there are a number of ways this can happen, like, for example, let's say you just installed, you had MDM enabled, and you just installed an enrollment profile on a device.
After the device got enrolled in Fleet, it would then...
And get a package to install the fleet agent.
That package would not have a specific team associated with it.
So it would get enrolled using the global enrollment secret into no team.
Now, strangely, no team is actually a team.
It's a team with team ID zero.
All the other teams have some other ID in the database.
No team just means it's used the global enrollment secret, and it doesn't really have a team association.
But if I go into a device, and this is what the device record looks like.
So it shows the device details, the software that's installed on the device, the queries for this device, and the policies that are associated to it.
There's a number of actions that I can perform over here.
One of them is I can transfer it to another team, which is pretty cool and super easy to do.
You can also do that with the API.
What a lot
What of customers have set up is an enrollment flow where every device gets enrolled into no team, and it's kind of functioning as like a staging team.
And then there's some automation that's performed to move it to the right team after it's been assessed.
And that just depends on how you're doing the enrollment.
It's flexible.
You can also run a script and a query.
I can't query this host because it's a VM, so it probably doesn't really even exist anymore.
But in order to run a live query, the device has to be actually talking to Fleet.
You can lock it or wipe it or delete it.
Now, delete means you're deleting the record.
But lock or wipe means you're actually wiping the device remotely or locking it.
These are very important security features.
One thing that's kind of cool about Fleet is that you'll see this lock and wipe regardless of what platform it is.
So this is a very important feature for us because it's difficult to manage lock and wipe.
For Linux, it's a little bit difficult to manage lock and wipe from Windows.
We have these actions available regardless of what platform the device is.
And they're all in the same place.
They function different ways, but the UI is consistent from platform to platform.
And that's a big theme for us.
It's like, how do we make managing multiple platforms flatter?
That's kind of the goal of the product, I would say, or one of the major goals of the product.
Okay, so let's actually get on a team and talk about controls.
So when we talk about controls, we're talking about things like OS updates.
And we have some of this built into the product.
So, like, you can set a minimum version for Mac OS.
You can set a deadline for Windows and the grace period.
You can set a minimum version for these other platforms.
You can also turn this off and you can add your own.
Software update controls, like Nudge, which is a very common one in the Mac management world.
We also have OS settings.
In OS settings, we have one OS setting built into the product, and that's disk encryption.

And this goes back to something that I said I would talk about before, which is that if I have a team, this isn't the team I was on before, I think it was Dale's team, that has multiple platforms, Macs and Windows.
What's kind of cool about having this disk encryption control built in is that I can set this single checkbox and turn on disk encryption for multiple platforms.
So if I have Linux computers in here, if I have Windows computers here, I have Macs in here.
Bye checkbox.
Check this checkbox.
Encryption is going to get enabled for Linux and Windows and Mac.
Now, encryption is very, very different on all three of those platforms.
It works differently completely on all three.
But I only have to set one control in Fleet to enable that if the team has multiple platforms.
So, again, this is kind of the theme, is this flattening simplification.
Not all of our customers need that.
Many of our customers don't need or even don't want easy buttons for things because they have sophisticated engineering teams and they want to manage this stuff on their own.
But this is something you can do.
Another thing you can do is have custom settings.
So this is device profiles.
So for Macs and iOS devices, these are referred to as configuration profiles.
For Windows, they're referred to as device profiles.
And these are just specifications on Windows and the Apple operating systems that allow you to.
So it's the things like, how long is the screen lock, and what shows up on the login screen?
Is it name and password?
And all those kinds of controls.
What's the password length?
All of the things that a security organization tells a client platform engineering organization that they have to set to be a certain way so that they can be compliant.
So this is a very important part of the product.
This tells you that the controls have been verified on hosts.
It tells you whether they're in a verifying state.
This is using OS Query to do that check.
And it's telling you if any have failed.
Okay?
Yep.
We have this end user setup experience.
And what that means is this is integrated to work with automated enrollment programs.
So for Mac, this is referred to as automated device enrollment.
And also for iOS devices.
For Windows, this is referred to as Autopilot or Automated Enrollment.
And what the end user experience is, is a thing that it's basically a fancy progress bar, right?
And lots of, Kanji has this similar feature, it's called Passport, which you probably saw when your computer was being set up the first time.
So there's like a screen that's showing on your computer that's kind of like telling you what's happening and what's being installed.
And then at the end of that process, your computer is set up.
So that's what the end user experience does.
It lets you customize the bootstrap package.
It lets you install software at that phase.
It lets you run scripts to do stuff to the computer during enrollment, which is often very important for customers.
It lets you manage the setup assistant steps, which if you just take a computer out of the box or an iOS device out of the box to set it up, there's a number of steps that you have to go through.
This lets you skip over those if you want to.
So that's what the end user set of experience is.
And again, going back to this theme of the team represents a set of management concepts, if you will, for managing a certain set of devices in your org.
You very well might want to have a different set of experience for team A versus team B.
Now, maybe you don't have that.
Maybe everybody in your organization needs and wants the same set of experience.
But if you wanted them to be different, like if you wanted one of the setup assistant steps to be different for the accounting team versus the development team, you can do that.
That's the idea here.
You can have scripts and you have a library of scripts.
You can see that you can upload Windows scripts, PS1, or shell scripts.
Fleet will also accept ZSH scripts as well.
And you can...
can click on these and actually read them, which is kind of nice.
You can actually see what the script is without having to download it.
And there's a batching feature now, so you can actually run the script on multiple computers at one time.
It tells you when they're scheduled, when they're set to be done, when the batches are complete.
Another thing that we have here is variables.
There aren't any, but what this is going to allow you to do is create a string in Fleet that can be used as a variable in a script or a configuration profile.
And there's lots of technical reasons why you might want to do that.
It's actually kind of cool that you can do this from the console.
I don't know that any other MDM has this feature.
But it's pretty nerdy and pretty technical.

And again, you...
can install software differently for every one of your teams.

The UI in the server looks very much like the UI that the end user sees.
And again, I'm probably boring you at this point.
But the idea is that fleet is transparent and flat.
That means that we're also as transparent as possible to the end user.
So, this is something that you would have to work pretty hard in Kanji or in Jamf to show the end user, hey, these are the policies that I'm running on your computer.

would fail, you just need to update one pass?

And I don't think...
You know, there's a couple more things to say about software.
We have fleet-maintained apps, which means that those can be automatically installed from fleet.
You can upload custom software if one of the fleet...
If you need to install something that we don't have in the fleet-maintained app.
library.
And that's pretty much it.
There's kind of three levels of software.
There's the entire catalog of things you could install.
There's the library.
That means that those are things that you've set to install.
And then there's the inventory.
That's the devices reporting back, showing what software they actually have.
And then from those three kind of collections, there's a few different ways to install software.
One is with a custom package.
Two is from an app store.
Three is with the fleet-maintained apps.
Make sense?
Yep.
And if I add software, this is what that looks like.
This is fleet-maintained apps.
This is stuff from the app store.
There probably isn't even the VPP token set up in this instance.
And then this is uploaded custom packages with which you can have
Custom install scripts, and you can set them for self-service, and set them for automatic install.
All good stuff.
Okay, so to your question, a policy in fleet is something pretty interesting, and it doesn't trigger automation, but a policy failure can, and that is the trigger for all automation in fleet.
Now, I think I mentioned this very briefly, but before I dive completely into policies, I want to go back here and talk about queries.
All of the fleet features, and MDM is a separate specification, but one of the interesting things about fleet as a product is that the MDM features are kind of built upon OS Query.
What is OS Query?
OS Query was a project that was started at Facebook in 2014.
One of our co-founders, actually, was one of the co-creators of the OS Query product.
Um...
whole...
Well, OS Query is open source.
There are lots of products that use OS Query now.
CrowdStrike uses it.
There are other products that have it built in.
None of those products have one of the co-founders as the co-founder of a product like this.
So a query just means that when you install the OS Query agent, and I guess I should back up a little bit because I've already talked about this.
When you enroll a computer in Fleet, you install what we refer to as Fleet D.
The Fleet D actually has two agents.
One is the OS Query agent.
Two is what's called Orbit, which is the Fleet agent that kind of does everything else.
So this would be the equivalent of the Jamf binary or the Congee binary.
Okay, OS Query is kind of a completely separate thing.
And
And what it was built to do is find data on an operating system, and it could be anything.
It could be the contents of a file.
It could be the output of a shell binary.
It could be using some deep-level API.
Basically, any information that you can get from the computer, and it expresses that data as a SQL table, a SQLite table.
So I don't know how much you know about databases or how much you care about databases.

But what's really cool about that is what that means is if you can express the data from a computer in the same way from every computer, you're kind of limiting what you have to know to collect that data.
In other words, if you can express the data on an operating system as a SQL table, that means you can write SQL queries against that SQL table to get the data back.
And that's one of the kind of core features of Fleet.
If I look at one of these queries, and I don't know that there are any saved in here.
Here we go.
This is a very, very simple query.
This is just saying from the OS query flags table, give me everything.
That's what select star means.
If you look at this list of tables over here, there's 344 different tables.
It's a long list.
And there's...
Yeah, think Sam, or somebody shared that list.
Cool.
The account policy data.
Yeah.
And there's, you know, there's 344 of them.
So, like, you can get Wi-Fi status.
You can get Windows crash log.
You can get Windows live events.
It's pretty incredible.
Let me show you in a little bit easier to read way.
Because that view of it is meant to be searchable.
Yeah.
So this is the Apple table, the Apple-specific tables, Linux-specific tables.
Now, there are some tables that are cross-platform, and the ones that tend to be our cross-platform are the ones where, like, why is the ARP cache table available on Apple, Windows, and Linux?
Well, it's because the modern network stack predates any of these operating systems, right?
The modern network stack goes all the way back to the original Unix computers, and the network stack really hasn't changed that much.
So the ARP cache is something that exists in the network stack of every operating system.
That's why this table is compatible with all three.
If that weren't the case, then Macs and Windows computers wouldn't be able to talk to each other across the network, right?
And this is just really basic networking information, but it's available in this table.
You can combine the tables.
So you could pass the information from one table to another.

So it's really the sky is the limit in terms of what kind of data you can collect as long as you can come up with a creative way for using these tables to express it and then parse it down with a query.

And this is part of the reason why people use Fleet alongside other MDMs is because this data is so rich and so expressive that you can enhance the management capabilities of any other platform with data like this.
Okay, now without, I'm not going to go any more into it than that.
You can do a lot with queries.
There's a lot of tables.
It's really cool.
Now, how does this work with policies?
Well, policies also use queries, but the difference is instead of trying to collect data, what you're trying to do with a policy query is just generate a Boolean result.
In other words, I'm assessing the state of something on the device and I'm finding out what.
Whether or not it's true or false.
If I can write a query in such a way that I can get a failure, like is my one password up to date, then that can act as a trigger in fleet for automation.
So when I go back here and select automations, this is basically saying, okay, these are the automations that are available to you if a policy fails.
One of the things I can do, which we've already talked about, I can send a generic webhook.
Again, this is incredibly powerful.
This means on any policy failure that I can generate, I can send a generic webhook to anything that's capable of listening to a webhook and trigger some other action in some other system.
Or I could send an API call back to fleet directly to do something else.
I can also send a ticket to a ticketing system like Jira or Zendesk.
If I don't use Jira or Zendesk, my ticketing system probably can handle a generic webhook.
So this this is...
Super powerful.
What else can I do?
I can run a script on a policy failure.
Why would I do that?
Kanji has a feature for automatic remediation.
Like if it runs a script and it determines that the state of that script isn't what it's supposed to be, it runs a script to fix that.
So you could get that same behavior with this.
Exactly the same.
In fact, I would argue that it's better because it's not running on a clock to check that one thing.
It's just checking the state of the device and only running it if the state of the device shows that it's failed.
